demographics.audit_notes: Values are plausible for a large aggregation of North Austin tracts in 1990. New fields are estimated from Census data. Rent burden data is not available for this period.
Values appear to be a reasonable interpolation between the 1990 and 2000 decennial censuses.

Corrected black_pct from 5.1% to 4.7% to better reflect census tract data for the area, which showed slower growth in that period. Adjusted other_pct to maintain 100% sum. Original values: black_pct=5.1, other_pct=0.6.
Original values are plausible interpolations. New fields are sourced from the first available ACS 5-year estimates, centered on 2007.
Values align well with 2010 Census and ACS 2006-2010 data for aggregated North Austin tracts.
Population growth is rapid but plausible for this period, reflecting major developments like The Domain. Values align with ACS 2011-2015 data.
Values align well with 2020 Census and ACS 2016-2020 data for aggregated North Austin tracts.
Values are plausible estimates based on ACS 2017-2021 data.
Values are plausible estimates based on the latest available ACS 2018-2022 data.
Values are projections based on recent ACS trends. Confidence is medium as they are not based on released data.
Values are projections based on recent ACS trends. Confidence is medium as they are not based on released data.
Values are projections based on recent ACS trends. Confidence is medium as they are not based on released data.

socioeconomic.audit_notes: Values align with 1990 Census. Several new fields are unavailable for this period.
Values are plausible interpolations reflecting economic growth in the mid-90s.
Values align with 2000 Census data at the peak of the dot-com boom.
Corrected unemployment rate from 5.5% to 4.5% to better align with Austin MSA data for 2005 from BLS. Original value was too high for a pre-recession year.
Values align with ACS data reflecting the impact of the Great Recession.
Values reflect strong economic recovery and growth in the tech sector.
Unemployment rate reflects the spike from the COVID-19 pandemic. Eviction rate is an estimate, likely lowered by pandemic-era moratoriums.
Values show continued income growth and labor market recovery post-2020.
Values based on latest available ACS data. Eviction rate estimate increased as moratoriums expired.
Projected values based on recent trends. Unemployment rate reflects slight uptick in local market.
Projected values based on recent trends.
Projected values based on recent trends.
